title: Normalizing Hidden Layers
concept: Batch normalization uses each mini-batch to recenter and rescale hidden activations so the next layer sees a more consistent range during training.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why can deeper layers struggle when earlier layers are still learning quickly?

options.text: Because the hidden activations feeding them keep changing shape and scale.
Because the labels disappear after the first batch.
Because neurons are only allowed to update once.

exercises.explain: Correct. Changing hidden activations make later layers learn on unstable inputs.

cards.title: Leveling the audio before the next speaker
cards.body: In a podcast studio, one guest whispers and another shouts. Before sending their voices to the recording system, the engineer levels the sound so the next device is not constantly shocked by huge volume changes. Batch norm does something similar for hidden activations inside a network.

cards.title: Center and scale the hidden activations
cards.body: Batch normalization looks at the activations in the current mini-batch, then recenters and rescales them before the next layer uses them. The goal is not to make every value identical. The goal is to give the next layer a more consistent, well-behaved range to learn from.

cards.title: Using measuring cups instead of random pours
cards.body: If one baker dumps in flour with a giant mug and the next uses a tiny cup, recipes become chaotic. Measuring cups make the ingredient amounts more consistent. Batch norm gives later layers a more measured input scale instead of random hidden-layer swings.

cards.title: Two batches, one calmer range
cards.body: Suppose one batch makes a hidden neuron fire mostly between 20 and 40, while the next batch makes it fire mostly between -5 and 3. Batch norm recenters and rescales those values so the following layer sees inputs that are easier to interpret from batch to batch.

cards.title: Not just input preprocessing
cards.body: Normalizing the original dataset before training is useful, but batch norm tackles a different place. It normalizes inside the network, between layers, where the shifting-input problem happens.

cards.title: Raw activations vs. normalized activations
cards.body: What the next layer experiences before and after batch norm:

left.text:
Activations may drift high or low.
Scale can vary a lot across batches.
Next layer keeps re-adjusting.

right.text:
Activations are re-centered and re-scaled.
Range is more consistent across batches.
Next layer gets steadier inputs.

exercises.prompt: What does batch normalization do before the next layer sees the activations?

options.text: It recenters and rescales the activations using the current batch.
It deletes every negative activation forever.
It replaces the labels with new ones.
It freezes all earlier layers so they never change again.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Batch norm uses the mini-batch to make the activations more centered and consistently scaled.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Batch normalization is mainly about making the next layer's job more stable.
exercises.explain: True. By calming the hidden activation range, batch norm gives later layers steadier inputs to learn from.

exercises.sentence: Batch normalization helps by centering and ___ hidden activations.
exercises.explain: Right. The usual intuition is centering plus scaling so later layers see a more predictable range.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario sounds most like batch normalization in action?

options.text: A hidden layer's outputs are adjusted within the current batch so the next layer receives a steadier range of values.
The network permanently removes a layer each time the loss rises.
A file server alphabetizes the training images on disk.

exercises.explain: Yes. Batch norm acts on hidden activations before they move forward, not on filenames or labels.

exercises.prompt: A deep network has one hidden layer whose outputs sometimes cluster around 30 and other times around -8, making the next layer hard to train. Why is batch normalization a good fit here?

options.text: Because it can recenter and rescale those hidden activations batch by batch before the next layer uses them.
Because it turns the whole model into a shallow network with no hidden layers.
Because it changes the true labels to match the shifting activations.
Because it prevents activations from ever changing during learning.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Batch norm does not stop learning — it makes the hidden activations easier for the next layer to handle. Next, we'll see why batch norm is flexible instead of rigid: it can learn how much to stretch and shift the values back.
